hypothesis: An equivariant graph neural network trained on the XDIP dataset to predict Fe K-edge XANES spectra will achieve significantly lower mean absolute error than non-equivariant GNN baselines (GCN, GAT, GIN) specifically for iron centers with low-symmetry coordination environments (e.g., distorted tetrahedral or mixed-ligand geometries), because explicitly encoding SE(3)-equivariant geometric features—such as bond angles and dihedral torsions around the iron center—will capture the orientation-dependent electronic structure contributions that flat graph representations discard, and this performance gap will be measurable and statistically significant on a held-out subset of XDIP entries stratified by coordination geometry class.
